examples.title: Basic Path Manipulation with pathlib
examples.description: Demonstrates fundamental path operations using pathlib
examples.code:
from pathlib import Path

# Create path objects
path = Path('/home/user/documents/project.py')

# Get path components
print(f"Name: {path.name}") # project.py
print(f"Stem: {path.stem}") # project
print(f"Suffix: {path.suffix}") # .py
print(f"Parent: {path.parent}") # /home/user/documents

# Join paths using / operator
new_path = Path('/home/user') / 'documents' / 'file.txt'
print(new_path) # /home/user/documents/file.txt

# Check path properties
print(path.exists()) # True/False
print(path.is_file()) # True/False
print(path.is_dir()) # True/False
print(path.is_absolute()) # True
examples.explanation: Pathlib provides an intuitive object-oriented interface for path manipulation. Key attributes like name, stem, suffix, and parent make path component extraction straightforward.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is path length

examples.title: Cross-Platform Path Joining
examples.description: Shows how to handle paths across different operating systems
examples.code:
import os
from pathlib import Path

# Using os.path (traditional approach)
parts = ['home', 'user', 'documents', 'file.txt']
joined = os.path.join(*parts)
print(joined) # home/user/file.txt (Unix) or home\user\file.txt (Windows)

# Using pathlib (modern approach)
path = Path('home') / 'user' / 'documents' / 'file.txt'
print(path) # home/user/file.txt (auto-adjusts per OS)

# Absolute path conversion
relative = Path('data/file.txt')
absolute = relative.absolute()
print(absolute) # /current/working/directory/data/file.txt

# Expand user home directory
user_path = Path('~/documents')
expanded = user_path.expanduser()
print(expanded) # /home/username/documents

# Resolve symlinks
symlink_path = Path('link_to_file')
resolved = symlink_path.resolve()
print(resolved) # Actual file location
examples.explanation: Both os.path and pathlib handle OS-specific separators automatically. The pathlib approach using the / operator is more readable and is the recommended modern approach.
examples.timeComplexity: O(k) where k is number of path components

examples.title: Directory Operations with pathlib
examples.code:
from pathlib import Path

# Create directory structure
base = Path('/tmp/project')
(base / 'data').mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
(base / 'logs').mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

# List all files in directory
for item in Path('.').iterdir():
 print(f"{item.name} - {'DIR' if item.is_dir() else 'FILE'}")

# Recursive search for .py files
py_files = list(Path('.').rglob('*.py'))
print(f"Found {len(py_files)} Python files")

# Glob pattern matching
text_files = list(Path('.').glob('**/*.txt'))
print(f"Found {len(text_files)} text files")

# Walk up the directory tree
current = Path('/home/user/documents/project/file.py')
for parent in current.parents:
 print(parent)
# /home/user/documents/project
# /home/user/documents
# /home/user
# /home
examples.explanation: pathlib provides powerful methods for directory operations. rglob() is particularly useful for recursive file searches, while parents provides easy traversal up the directory tree.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for directory listing, O(n*m) for glob where m is depth

examples.title: File Information and Modification
examples.description: Get and modify file metadata using pathlib
examples.code:
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime

path = Path('example.txt')

# Get file stats
if path.exists():
 stat = path.stat()
 print(f"Size: {stat.st_size} bytes")
 print(f"Modified: {datetime.fromtimestamp(stat.st_mtime)}")
 print(f"Created: {datetime.fromtimestamp(stat.st_ctime)}")
 print(f"Permissions: {oct(stat.st_mode)[-3:]}")

# File operations
new_path = path.with_name('new_example.txt')
path.rename(new_path)

# Change suffix
txt_path = path.with_suffix('.md')

# Read/Write files
path.write_text("Hello, World!")
content = path.read_text()

# Remove file
path.unlink() # Delete file
# path.rmdir() # Delete empty directory
examples.explanation: pathlib integrates file operations with path objects. stat() provides detailed file metadata. Methods like with_name(), with_suffix(), rename(), and unlink() enable file manipulation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for stat, O(n) for read/write where n is file size

examples.title: Path Validation Utility
examples.description: Comprehensive path validation and error handling
examples.explanation: This utility demonstrates proper error handling for path operations. It uses exception handling for robustness and provides comprehensive path analysis including type detection, symlink resolution, and metadata retrieval.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of directory entries
